main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Active Test Time Prompt Learning (ATPL) for Vision-Language Models (VLMs)","Test-time prompt tuning with actively sampled labels","Dynamically adjusted uncertainty threshold for querying labels at test time","Class-balanced annotation buffer with replacement of non-informative samples","Class-aware distribution alignment for adaptation tasks"],"problems":["Improving generalization and performance of VLMs in downstream tasks when labeled training data is unavailable at deployment","Efficiently adapting VLMs at test time with minimal annotation cost","Mitigating risk and uncertainty in VLM predictions at test time","Addressing the challenge of single-sample, streaming test-time adaptation with active learning"],"datasets":["10 cross-dataset transfer datasets (specific names not provided)","4 domain-generalisation datasets (specific names not provided)"],"metrics":["Accuracy","Annotation budget (number of queried labels)","Buffer capacity (number of stored labeled samples)"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art","value":"Consistent improvement across all 10 cross-dataset transfer and 4 domain-generalisation datasets (exact numbers not provided)"},{"metric":"Annotation budget","value":"Low annotation budget required for superior performance (exact numbers not provided)"},{"metric":"Buffer capacity","value":"Limited buffer capacity used while outperforming other methods (exact numbers not provided)"}],"novelty_claims":["First to apply Active Learning in a Test Time setting for Vision-Language Models (VLMs)","Propose a dynamically adjusted threshold for querying uncertain test samples in a streaming, single-sample setting","Introduce class balancing and non-informative sample replacement in the annotation buffer for test-time adaptation","Propose class-aware distribution alignment using actively labeled samples for fine-grained adaptation","Define a new, fair evaluation protocol for this novel problem setting"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PCB (Prompt Class Balancing) algorithm: a novel active learning framework for Vision Language Models (VLMs) that addresses class imbalance by leveraging pre-trained VLM knowledge and integrates with conventional active learning techniques"],"problems":["How to effectively adapt pre-trained Vision Language Models (VLMs) to new tasks with minimal labeled data","The challenge of high labeling costs for adapting VLMs","Performance degradation of conventional active learning methods when applied to VLMs due to class imbalance in selected samples"],"datasets":["Caltech101","Oxford-IIIT Pets","EuroSAT","Describable Textures Dataset (DTD)","Flower102","(and three more real-world datasets, but only these are named explicitly)"],"metrics":["Accuracy","Imbalance (variance of the number of samples among classes)"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy (PCB vs. random/conventional AL)","value":"PCB consistently outperforms both random sampling and conventional active learning methods across all evaluated datasets"},{"metric":"Imbalance (variance of samples among classes)","value":"PCB achieves lower class imbalance compared to conventional active learning methods"}],"novelty_claims":["First exploration of synergistic approaches to active learning and Vision Language Models (VLMs)","Identification and analysis of the class imbalance problem when applying conventional active learning to VLMs","Introduction of the PCB algorithm, which uses pre-trained VLM knowledge to balance class selection in active learning, leading to improved performance with limited labeled data","Demonstration that naïve application of active learning to VLMs can degrade performance, and that PCB overcomes this issue"]}

methods: PCB (Prompt Class Balancing) algorithm: a novel active learning framework for Vision Language Models (VLMs) that addresses class imbalance by leveraging pre-trained VLM knowledge and integrates with conventional active learning techniques
problems: How to effectively adapt pre-trained Vision Language Models (VLMs) to new tasks with minimal labeled data
The challenge of high labeling costs for adapting VLMs
Performance degradation of conventional active learning methods when applied to VLMs due to class imbalance in selected samples
datasets: (and three more real-world datasets, but only these are named explicitly)
metrics: Imbalance (variance of the number of samples among classes)

results.value: PCB consistently outperforms both random sampling and conventional active learning methods across all evaluated datasets
PCB achieves lower class imbalance compared to conventional active learning methods

novelty_claims: First exploration of synergistic approaches to active learning and Vision Language Models (VLMs)
Identification and analysis of the class imbalance problem when applying conventional active learning to VLMs
Introduction of the PCB algorithm, which uses pre-trained VLM knowledge to balance class selection in active learning, leading to improved performance with limited labeled data
Demonstration that naïve application of active learning to VLMs can degrade performance, and that PCB overcomes this issue

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Calibrated Test-time Prompt Tuning (C-TPT): a method for optimizing prompts during test-time to improve calibration by maximizing Average Text Feature Dispersion (ATFD) in CLIP models","Definition and use of Average Text Feature Dispersion (ATFD) as a calibration-related metric for prompt selection and optimization"],"problems":["Improving calibration (quantifying prediction uncertainty) in test-time prompt tuning for vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) without using labeled data","Addressing overconfidence and poor calibration in CLIP models when using test-time prompt tuning, which traditionally focuses only on accuracy"],"datasets":["Multiple datasets used for CLIP evaluation (exact datasets not specified in the provided content, but described as 'various datasets')"],"metrics":["Expected Calibration Error (ECE)","Accuracy","Average Text Feature Dispersion (ATFD)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["First to address calibration in test-time prompt tuning for vision-language models like CLIP, without requiring labeled data","Establishes and quantifies a strong negative correlation between Average Text Feature Dispersion (ATFD) and Expected Calibration Error (ECE) in CLIP models","Proposes C-TPT, a novel method that optimizes prompts at test time to improve calibration by maximizing ATFD, leading to better-calibrated predictions across various datasets and CLIP architectures"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["MeanShift for Test-time Augmentation (MTA): a robust, training-free, plug-and-play method that leverages multiple augmented views of an image and optimizes an inlierness score for each view via a density mode seeking process"],"problems":["Improving zero-shot generalization of vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) at test time","Reducing reliance on prompt learning/tuning, which requires heavy training and is impractical for black-box/API-based models","Enhancing test-time augmentation without ad hoc rules or hyperparameter tuning"],"datasets":["15 datasets (not individually named in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Zero-shot classification accuracy","Few-shot classification accuracy","Computational efficiency (time and memory)"],"results":[{"metric":"Zero-shot classification accuracy (average over 15 datasets)","value":"MTA surpasses state-of-the-art prompt-tuning alternatives (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"},{"metric":"Computational efficiency","value":"MTA is more time and memory efficient than prompt-tuning methods"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a robust MeanShift-based test-time augmentation (MTA) that does not require prompt learning or training, making it suitable for black-box/API-based models","Introduces an inlierness score for each augmented view, optimized jointly with the density mode seeking process, eliminating the need for ad hoc thresholds or hyperparameter tuning","Demonstrates that MTA can be used as a plug-and-play module on top of zero-shot and few-shot models, consistently improving performance","Shows that MTA works in a training-free and hyperparameter-free manner, unlike previous test-time augmentation or prompt-tuning methods"]}

methods: MeanShift for Test-time Augmentation (MTA): a robust, training-free, plug-and-play method that leverages multiple augmented views of an image and optimizes an inlierness score for each view via a density mode seeking process
problems: Improving zero-shot generalization of vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) at test time

methods: Conditional Context Optimization (CoCoOp): a method that extends Context Optimization (CoOp) by learning a lightweight neural network to generate input-conditional tokens (vectors) for each image, resulting in instance-conditional prompts.
Context Optimization (CoOp): a method that turns context words in a prompt into a set of learnable vectors for adapting pre-trained vision-language models.
problems: Generalizability of learned prompts in vision-language models to unseen classes within the same dataset (overfitting to base classes).
Efficient adaptation of large pre-trained vision-language models (like CLIP) to downstream datasets without full model fine-tuning.
Improving transferability and domain generalization of prompt learning methods.
datasets: 11 image recognition datasets (not all named in the excerpt, but includes datasets for scene recognition and other visual recognition tasks).
Specific examples mentioned: datasets with classes such as 'arrival gate', 'cathedral', 'wind farm', 'train railway', and a 'pet' dataset.
metrics: Domain generalization performance (likely measured by accuracy or similar metrics)
novelty_claims: Introduction of conditional prompt learning for vision-language models, where prompts are conditioned on each input instance rather than being static.
Proposal of Conditional Context Optimization (CoCoOp), which uses a lightweight neural network to generate input-conditional tokens for prompts, improving generalizability and transferability.
Demonstration that instance-conditional prompts are more robust to class shift and generalize better to unseen classes and domains than static, learned prompts.

Unified context: sharing the same context across all classes
Class-specific context: learning a specific set of context tokens for each class
problems: The need for manual prompt engineering in deploying vision-language models like CLIP, which is time-consuming and requires domain expertise
Suboptimal performance and inefficiency due to hand-crafted prompts in downstream tasks

results.metric: Average gain over hand-crafted prompts with 16 shots
Maximum gain over hand-crafted prompts with 16 shots

novelty_claims: First to automate prompt engineering for vision-language models using continuous prompt learning (CoOp)
Propose two implementations (unified and class-specific context) for different recognition tasks
Show that prompt learning-based approach (CoOp) outperforms both handcrafted prompts and linear probe models in downstream transfer learning and domain generalization
Demonstrate that CoOp is data-efficient, requiring as few as 1-2 shots to surpass hand-crafted prompts
Show that CoOp is robust to domain shifts compared to zero-shot models using manual prompts

methods: Visual Prompt-based Test-Time Adaptation (VPTTA)
Low-frequency prompt for each test image
Memory bank for prompt initialization based on similarity of low-frequency components
Statistics-fusion-based warm-up mechanism for prompt training
problems: Distribution shift in medical image segmentation across different centers
Error accumulation and catastrophic forgetting in continual test-time adaptation (CTTA)
Inefficiency and unreliability of updating pretrained models during test-time adaptation
datasets: Not explicitly mentioned in the provided content, but refers to two medical image segmentation benchmark tasks
metrics: Not explicitly mentioned in the provided content, but likely includes standard segmentation metrics such as Dice coefficient, IoU, etc.
novelty_claims: Proposes a method (VPTTA) that adapts to each test image by training a lightweight, sample-specific visual prompt instead of updating the model parameters.
Introduces the low-frequency prompt, which targets the style-related components most responsible for distribution shift, and is efficient to train (single iteration, few parameters).
Utilizes a memory bank to initialize prompts based on similarity to previous test images, improving adaptation efficiency and effectiveness.
Designs a statistics-fusion-based warm-up mechanism to facilitate prompt training under large distribution shifts.
Demonstrates that the method avoids error accumulation and catastrophic forgetting common in continual test-time adaptation.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Test-time Personalized Gaze estimation (TPGaze) method","Prompt tuning: updating a small set of prompt parameters at test time while freezing the backbone network","Meta-learning to initialize the prompt so that unsupervised loss minimization aligns with minimizing gaze estimation error","Use of unsupervised losses (e.g., rotation consistency loss, left-right symmetry loss) for adaptation"],"problems":["Efficient and accurate personalization of gaze estimation at test time without labels","Performance degradation of gaze estimation models under distribution shift in real-world scenarios","Practical constraints of adapting all model parameters on edge devices at test time","Lack of source data for unsupervised domain adaptation due to privacy concerns"],"datasets":["Four cross-dataset validations (exact dataset names not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Gaze estimation error","Adaptation speed (e.g., time to adapt)","Number of tunable parameters required for personalization"],"results":[{"metric":"Gaze estimation error (average over four cross-dataset validations)","value":"Lowest among compared methods (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"Adaptation speed","value":"At least 10 times faster than compared methods"},{"metric":"Tunable parameters for personalization","value":"Less than 1% of ResNet-18 parameters"}],"novelty_claims":["First to propose efficient test-time personalization for gaze estimation using prompt tuning, updating less than 1% of parameters.","Meta-learned prompt initialization ensures that unsupervised loss minimization at test time aligns with minimizing gaze estimation error.","Demonstrates that prompt tuning enables much faster adaptation (at least 10x) compared to existing methods, with superior accuracy."]}

methods: Test-time Personalized Gaze estimation (TPGaze) method
Prompt tuning: updating a small set of prompt parameters at test time while freezing the backbone network
Meta-learning to initialize the prompt so that unsupervised loss minimization aligns with minimizing gaze estimation error
Use of unsupervised losses (e.g., rotation consistency loss, left-right symmetry loss) for adaptation

Less than 1% of ResNet-18 parameters

novelty_claims: First to propose efficient test-time personalization for gaze estimation using prompt tuning, updating less than 1% of parameters.

Demonstrates that prompt tuning enables much faster adaptation (at least 10x) compared to existing methods, with superior accuracy.

methods: DPCore (Dynamic Prompt Coreset)
Visual Prompt Adaptation
Prompt Coreset
Dynamic Update mechanism
problems: Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) under dynamic, real-world domain changes (Continual Dynamic Change, CDC)

novelty_claims: Introduction of the Continual Dynamic Change (CDC) setting for CTTA, reflecting real-world domain shifts with varying frequencies and durations.
Proposal of DPCore, a dynamic CTTA method that uses a prompt coreset to preserve, update, and expand domain knowledge efficiently.
DPCore achieves state-of-the-art results in both classification and segmentation under both structured (CSC) and dynamic (CDC) settings.
DPCore is highly computationally efficient, reducing trainable parameters by 99% and computation time by 64% compared to previous methods.
DPCore eliminates the need for expensive warm-up procedures and uses only 1% of ViDA’s parameters.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Marginal Entropy Minimization with One test point (MEMO): At test time, perform multiple data augmentations on a single test input, pass these through a probabilistic, adaptable model, and adapt all model parameters by minimizing the entropy of the model's average (marginal) output distribution across augmentations."],"problems":["Improving robustness of deep neural networks to distribution shift at test time, especially when only a single test input is available.","Developing test time adaptation methods that do not require access to multiple test points or specialized training procedures."],"datasets":["ImageNet-C","ImageNet-R","ImageNet-A"],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy improvement on ImageNet-C and ImageNet-R","value":"1-8% over standard model evaluation"},{"metric":"Accuracy improvement on ImageNet-A (ResNet-50)","value":"1-4% over standard model evaluation"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes MEMO, a test time adaptation method that requires only a single test input and no assumptions about model training or architecture.","MEMO is the first adaptation method to improve performance on the ImageNet-A test set for ResNet-50 models.","MEMO is broadly applicable as a 'plug and play' method for pretrained models and test settings, and synergizes with other robustification techniques.","MEMO achieves state-of-the-art results on ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, and ImageNet-A in the single test point setting."]}

methods: Marginal Entropy Minimization with One test point (MEMO): At test time, perform multiple data augmentations on a single test input, pass these through a probabilistic, adaptable model, and adapt all model parameters by minimizing the entropy of the model's average (marginal) output distribution across augmentations.
problems: Improving robustness of deep neural networks to distribution shift at test time, especially when only a single test input is available.
Developing test time adaptation methods that do not require access to multiple test points or specialized training procedures.

results.metric: Accuracy improvement on ImageNet-C and ImageNet-R
results.value: 1-8% over standard model evaluation
1-4% over standard model evaluation

novelty_claims: Proposes MEMO, a test time adaptation method that requires only a single test input and no assumptions about model training or architecture.
MEMO is the first adaptation method to improve performance on the ImageNet-A test set for ResNet-50 models.
MEMO is broadly applicable as a 'plug and play' method for pretrained models and test settings, and synergizes with other robustification techniques.
MEMO achieves state-of-the-art results on ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, and ImageNet-A in the single test point setting.
